events.title: Trump Signs Space Policy Directive 1
events.detail: President Trump signed SPD-1, "Reinvigorating America's Human Space Exploration Program," officially redirecting NASA back to the Moon. It amended the 2010 National Space Policy, tasking NASA to lead a program to return humans to the Moon and eventually reach Mars. This act marked the official genesis of what would become the Artemis program.

events.title: NASA Announces 'Moon to Mars' Campaign and Deep Space Gateway
events.detail: NASA formally branded its exploration strategy as 'Moon to Mars' and announced the Deep Space Gateway — a proposed small station in lunar orbit. In the FY2019 budget request, the name shifted to Lunar Orbital Platform-Gateway (LOP-G), signaling a structured international partnership model. International partners ESA, JAXA, and CSA expressed interest in contributing hardware.

sources.name: Planetary Society Moon to Mars Overview

events.title: VP Pence Issues 2024 Moon Landing Mandate
events.detail: Vice President Mike Pence, speaking at the fifth National Space Council meeting, directed NASA to land American astronauts on the Moon within five years — by 2024 — four years ahead of schedule. He declared NASA would use 'any means necessary,' including commercial rockets, and warned that contractors unable to meet the goal would be replaced. This political mandate became the forcing function for the entire accelerated Artemis program.

events.title: NASA Names Lunar Return Program 'Artemis'
events.detail: NASA Administrator Jim Bridenstine officially named the lunar return program 'Artemis,' after the Greek goddess twin sister of Apollo and goddess of the Moon. The name carried a deliberate commitment to land the first woman on the Moon. Bridenstine set 2024 as the target for the crewed lunar landing, with commercial and international partners as core elements of the architecture.

events.title: NASA Awards 11 HLS Study Contracts Under NextSTEP
events.detail: NASA awarded 11 study contracts under the Next Space Technologies for Exploration Partnerships (NextSTEP) program for the Human Landing System. These initial studies began the formal commercial lunar lander competition that would eventually lead to the SpaceX and Blue Origin contracts. Industry teams explored crewed lunar descent and ascent vehicles capable of landing near the south pole.

events.title: NASA Awards HLS Phase 1 Contracts ($967M Total)
events.detail: NASA selected three industry teams for 10-month HLS design contracts totaling ~$967 million: Blue Origin's National Team (with Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, Draper, and L3Harris), Dynetics (with Sierra Nevada), and SpaceX's Starship variant. Each team would develop full-scale lander concepts before a final down-select to an operational contract.

events.title: Northrop Grumman Wins Gateway HALO Design Contract ($187M)
events.detail: NASA signed a $187 million contract with Northrop Grumman to complete the preliminary design of HALO (Habitation and Logistics Outpost), Gateway's pressurized crew habitat. NASA also announced that HALO would be co-manifested with Maxar's Power and Propulsion Element (PPE) on a single Falcon Heavy, saving the cost of a separate rocket. This established the combined PPE+HALO as Gateway's first element.

events.title: Artemis Accords Signed by 8 Founding Nations
events.detail: NASA and the U.S. State Department launched the Artemis Accords with 8 founding nations: Australia, Canada, Italy, Japan, Luxembourg, UAE, UK, and the United States. The non-binding accords established principles for peaceful, transparent, and sustainable space exploration including interoperability, deconfliction of activities, and information sharing. China and Russia notably declined to sign, framing their own lunar programs outside the U.S.-led framework.

events.title: Trump Signs SPD-6: Space Nuclear Power & Propulsion Strategy
events.detail: President Trump signed Space Policy Directive 6, directing NASA and the Department of Energy to develop a fission surface power system demonstrable on the Moon by 2027 and scalable for Mars. SPD-6 also covered nuclear thermal propulsion for future deep-space missions. NASA Administrator Bridenstine called nuclear power 'fundamentally enabling' for sustained lunar presence and eventual Mars missions.

events.title: SLS Green Run Hot Fire Test 1 — Partial (67 Seconds)
events.detail: At Stennis Space Center, engineers ignited all four SLS RS-25 engines simultaneously for the first time, but the test was aborted at 67.7 seconds (target: ~500 seconds) when Engine #4 triggered a Major Component Failure indication. Investigation traced the abort to a conservatively set hydraulic parameter in the test stand, not an actual engine defect. A second full-duration test was immediately planned.

sources.name: American Astronautical Society Green Run Report

events.title: SLS Green Run Hot Fire Test 2 — Full Success (499 Seconds)
events.detail: The second Green Run static fire at Stennis Space Center B-2 test stand ran the full 8 minutes 19 seconds (499 seconds), firing all four RS-25 engines and generating 1.6 million pounds of thrust. All secondary objectives — engine gimballing, throttle profiles — were met. SLS Program Manager John Honeycutt declared the core stage earned 'an A-plus.' The test cleared SLS to ship to Kennedy Space Center for flight processing.

events.title: SpaceX Wins Sole HLS Contract — $2.89 Billion
events.detail: NASA selected only SpaceX's Starship HLS for the full Human Landing System development contract, valued at $2.89 billion. NASA stated it preferred two contractors but Congress had not appropriated sufficient funds. The contract covered one uncrewed lunar landing demonstration and one crewed Artemis III landing. Blue Origin and Dynetics were not selected, triggering immediate protest actions by both firms.

events.title: Blue Origin Files GAO Protest and Federal Lawsuit Over HLS
events.detail: Blue Origin and Dynetics protested the sole-source HLS award to the GAO on April 26. After GAO denied both protests in July 2021, Blue Origin sued NASA in the U.S. Court of Federal Claims in August. NASA paused SpaceX HLS work while litigation proceeded. Jeff Bezos personally offered over $3 billion to NASA to secure a second lander contract. A federal judge dismissed Blue Origin's suit on November 4, and SpaceX work resumed.

events.title: SLS Core Stage Arrives at Kennedy Space Center
events.detail: After shipping from Stennis Space Center, the SLS core stage arrived at KSC for vehicle integration in the Vehicle Assembly Building. By June 12, NASA announced the fully stacked Artemis I SLS/Orion vehicle assembly was complete, with the 322-foot rocket ready for further pre-launch processing. This was the first fully assembled Moon rocket at KSC since Apollo 17 in 1972.

events.title: SLS Exploration Upper Stage Passes Critical Design Review
events.detail: The Critical Design Review for the Exploration Upper Stage (EUS) was completed, clearing SLS Block 1B development to proceed to hardware fabrication. The EUS, powered by four RL10C-3 engines, would increase SLS payload capacity from 27 to 38 metric tons to trans-lunar injection — enabling Orion to carry co-manifested Gateway payloads. The EUS cost, originally $962M in 2017, would later balloon to nearly $2.8 billion before cancellation in 2026.

events.title: Artemis I Rolls Out to Launch Pad 39B for First Time
events.detail: The fully stacked Artemis I SLS/Orion vehicle — 322 feet tall, 5.75 million pounds — rolled out of the VAB to Launch Pad 39B over approximately 11 hours on the crawler-transporter. This was the first Moon rocket rollout since Apollo 17 in December 1972. The milestone began the Artemis I launch campaign and opened the wet dress rehearsal phase.

events.title: Three Wet Dress Rehearsal Attempts Face Propellant Leak Issues
events.detail: NASA attempted three wet dress rehearsals between April and May 2022, each encountering problems: a liquid hydrogen leak on April 1 cut short the first attempt; propellant loading cutoff on April 14 ended the second; an unresolved hydrogen leak ended the third. The rocket was rolled back to the VAB for repairs, delaying launch from spring to late summer/fall 2022. A successful full WDR was finally completed on June 20, 2022.

events.title: Artemis I Launch Scrub #1 — Faulty Engine Temperature Sensor
events.detail: The first Artemis I launch attempt was scrubbed at T-40 minutes due to a faulty temperature sensor on RS-25 Engine #3, which appeared not to have reached its required pre-chill temperature. Engineers determined the sensor itself was faulty, not the engine, but could not safely proceed. A second launch attempt was scheduled for September 3.

events.title: Artemis I Launch Scrub #2 — Liquid Hydrogen Leak
events.detail: The second launch attempt was scrubbed when a liquid hydrogen leak was detected at the quick-disconnect fitting on the core stage engine section. Teams attempted three remediation methods — warming, chilling, and helium repressurization — all failed to seal the leak. Launch Director Charlie Blackwell-Thompson called the scrub at 11:17 AM EDT. The vehicle was subsequently rolled back to the VAB for repairs.

sources.name: CBS News Artemis I September Scrub

events.title: Artemis I Launches — First SLS/Orion Flight
events.detail: Artemis I lifted off from Launch Complex 39B at 01:47:44 UTC (8:47 PM EST November 15), generating approximately 8.8 million pounds of thrust — 15% more than Saturn V. Ten CubeSats were deployed after trans-lunar injection. This was the first flight of SLS, the first integrated flight of SLS and Orion together, and the first NASA deep-space human-rated spacecraft launch since Apollo 17 in December 1972.

events.title: Orion Performs First Powered Lunar Flyby — 80 Miles from Surface
events.detail: Orion performed its first powered flyby of the Moon, passing within 80 miles (130 km) of the lunar surface. The flyby burn inserted the capsule into a Distant Retrograde Orbit around the Moon. This was the closest any human-rated spacecraft had come to the Moon since the Apollo 17 command module in 1972.

events.title: Orion Sets Distance Record — 268,563 Miles from Earth
events.detail: Orion reached its farthest point from Earth: 268,563 miles (432,210 km), setting a distance record for any spacecraft designed to carry humans — surpassing the Apollo 13 record. The spacecraft had been in a Distant Retrograde Orbit around the Moon for six days at this point. All systems performed nominally in the deep-space radiation environment.

events.title: Artemis I Splashdown — Mission Complete (25.5 Days, 1.4M Miles)
events.detail: Orion splashed down in the Pacific Ocean west of Baja California at 9:40 AM PST — exactly 50 years to the day after Apollo 17's Moon landing. The mission covered 2.3 million kilometers (1.4 million miles) over 25.5 days. Post-recovery inspections later revealed unexpected charring on the Avcoat heat shield, triggering a major investigation that would delay Artemis II by over a year.

events.title: Blue Origin Wins Second HLS Contract — $3.4 Billion for Blue Moon Mk.2
events.detail: Under the Sustaining Lunar Development (SLD) program, NASA awarded Blue Origin a $3.4 billion firm-fixed-price contract to develop the Blue Moon Mk.2 crewed lander for Artemis V. This gave NASA two competing HLS providers. Blue Moon must complete an uncrewed landing demonstration before any crewed Artemis V mission. The contract ended the sole-source SpaceX arrangement and increased NASA's architecture redundancy.

events.title: Artemis II Crew Announced: Wiseman, Glover, Koch, Hansen
events.detail: NASA Administrator Bill Nelson announced the four-person Artemis II crew at Ellington Field, Houston: Commander Reid Wiseman (NASA), Pilot Victor Glover (NASA, first person of color to fly to deep space), Mission Specialist Christina Koch (NASA, first woman on a lunar trajectory), and Mission Specialist Jeremy Hansen (CSA, first non-American on a lunar trajectory and Hansen's first spaceflight). The crew would fly a 10-day crewed lunar flyby mission.

events.title: India Signs Artemis Accords During Modi State Visit
events.detail: During Prime Minister Modi's state visit to Washington, India became a signatory of the Artemis Accords — a significant diplomatic win given ISRO's growing capabilities. India's inclusion strengthened the accords as a counterweight to China's space ambitions and reflected the broader Indo-US strategic partnership. The accords had grown to over 26 signatories by this point.

events.title: NASA Launches 121-Test Heat Shield Investigation at Ames
events.detail: Following post-flight inspections of the Artemis I Orion capsule revealing unexpected charring and material loss on the Avcoat heat shield, NASA launched an eight-campaign, 121-test investigation at NASA Ames Arc Jet Complex. Root cause was eventually traced to lower-than-expected heating rates during skip reentry, which allowed gases to build up in the Avcoat layers, causing cracking and shedding. The investigation ran through December 2023 and directly drove Artemis II delays.

events.title: Starship Reaches Orbital Trajectory on IFT-3 — Key HLS Milestone
events.detail: SpaceX's Starship/Super Heavy reached its target orbital trajectory for the first time on Integrated Flight Test 3 (IFT-3). A critical Artemis milestone was achieved during this flight: Starship performed an internal cryogenic propellant transfer between tanks — a precursor to the ship-to-ship orbital refueling required for HLS operations. An estimated 14+ tanker flights would be needed to fuel Starship HLS for a lunar mission.

events.title: Artemis II Delayed to Late 2025; Artemis III Pushed to 2026
events.detail: NASA officially announced the first major schedule slip: Artemis II pushed from late 2024 to 'no earlier than September 2025,' primarily due to the heat shield char loss requiring trajectory redesign and Orion battery issues — four Eaglepicher 120V Li-ion batteries required disassembly and replacement to tolerate abort system environments. Twenty-four electrical power anomalies traced to radiation effects required software workarounds. Artemis III was pushed from 2025 to late 2026.

sources.name: NASA OIG Artemis II Readiness Report May 2024

events.title: NASA Cancels VIPER Lunar Rover Despite Near-Completion
events.detail: NASA cancelled the Volatiles Investigating Polar Exploration Rover (VIPER) despite the rover being nearly complete, citing cost overruns and schedule delays. VIPER was designed to prospect for water ice at the lunar south pole — directly supporting Artemis surface operations. The cancellation drew sharp criticism from the planetary science community, who viewed it as prioritizing the crewed Artemis architecture over science. The completed rover was subsequently offered for sale.

events.title: Artemis II Delayed Again to April 2026; Artemis III to Mid-2027
events.detail: NASA announced a second major delay. Artemis II was pushed from September 2025 to April 2026. Artemis III was pushed from late 2026 to mid-2027. The root cause of the heat shield anomaly was confirmed: skip reentry's lower-than-expected heating rates allowed gas pressure buildup in Avcoat, causing cracking. NASA's resolution was to modify the reentry trajectory rather than replace the heat shield material — a significant design change.

events.title: Artemis Accords Surpass 50 Signatory Nations
events.detail: The Artemis Accords surpassed 50 signatory nations, with representatives across Europe, Asia, South America, Africa, and Oceania. Notable additions included Rwanda and Nigeria (first African nations in 2022), France, Saudi Arabia, and India. By January 2026, Oman's accession brought the total to 61 nations. China and Russia remained outside the framework, advancing their own lunar exploration program independently.

events.title: Blue Origin's New Glenn Reaches Orbit on Maiden Flight
events.detail: Blue Origin's New Glenn heavy-lift rocket launched from Cape Canaveral Launch Complex 36 at 2:03 AM EST, carrying the Blue Ring Pathfinder payload to medium Earth orbit. New Glenn reached orbit on its first attempt — a rare achievement for a new rocket. The first stage booster was lost on the descent landing attempt at approximately Mach 5.5. The successful debut bolstered Blue Origin's credentials as a viable HLS provider for Artemis V.

events.title: Trump FY2026 Budget Proposes 24% NASA Cut — Largest in History
events.detail: The White House FY2026 budget request proposed cutting NASA from $24.8 billion to $18.8 billion — a $6 billion, 24.3% reduction and the largest single-year cut in NASA history. Key proposals: cancel SLS and Orion after Artemis III; cancel Gateway; cut science by 47%. A new 'Commercial Moon to Mars Infrastructure' program at $864 million was proposed as a commercial-first replacement. Both House and Senate appropriations committees ultimately rejected the most severe cuts.

events.title: Senate Confirms Jared Isaacman as 15th NASA Administrator
events.detail: The Senate confirmed Jared Isaacman — billionaire entrepreneur and Crew Dragon commander of Inspiration4 and Polaris Dawn — as the 15th NASA Administrator with a 67-30 vote. Sworn in on December 18, Isaacman became one of the few NASA administrators with personal spaceflight experience. He immediately initiated an architecture review of the Artemis program, flagging unsustainable costs and schedule overruns.

events.title: Artemis II Crew Practices Full Launch Countdown at KSC
events.detail: The four Artemis II astronauts — Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen — practiced the full launch countdown procedure at Kennedy Space Center as part of final crew readiness preparations for the April 2026 launch target. This included suit-up, ingress drills, and communications checks in the Orion capsule atop the SLS rocket.

sources.name: Spaceflight Now Artemis II Countdown Practice

events.title: Artemis II Delayed Again by Liquid Hydrogen Leak During WDR
events.detail: During a pre-launch wet dress rehearsal tanking test, a liquid hydrogen fuel leak was discovered at the base of SLS. Additional issues included a faulty valve on Orion, audio communication problems, and cold-weather camera failures. Launch was pushed from late February to March or later, adding to the growing list of Artemis II delays. SLS Program Manager John Honeycutt noted the 3+ year gap between launches increased the risk of encountering issues.

sources.name: Aerospace America Artemis II WDR Issues

events.title: SLS Rolled Back to VAB for Helium Supply Line Blockage
events.detail: NASA's crawler-transporter 2 returned the fully stacked Artemis II SLS/Orion to the Vehicle Assembly Building to troubleshoot a blockage in the helium supply line to the ICPS (Interim Cryogenic Propulsion Stage) upper stage. The rollback effectively ruled out any March launch windows. A new fueling test was planned upon the vehicle's return to the pad, with an April or later target for launch.

sources.name: Spaceflight Now Artemis II VAB Rollback

events.title: NASA Cancels Exploration Upper Stage; ULA Centaur V Selected
events.detail: NASA announced the cancellation of the Exploration Upper Stage and the SLS Block 1B and Block 2 upgrade programs. SLS will continue in a standardized Block 1 configuration. NASA confirmed a contract with United Launch Alliance to use the Centaur V as a replacement upper stage for future SLS flights, formally confirmed by contract on March 7, 2026. This ended Boeing's multi-year EUS development program and wrote off approximately $2.8 billion in development investment.

sources.name: Spaceflight Now ULA Centaur V Contract

events.title: Isaacman Announces Major Artemis Architecture Overhaul
events.detail: NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman announced a sweeping restructuring of the Artemis program: Artemis III (now targeting 2027) redefined as a low-Earth orbit rendezvous and docking test — NOT a Moon landing; Artemis IV (targeting 2028) designated as the first actual crewed lunar landing; at least one surface landing per year thereafter. The Lunar Gateway was cancelled from early Artemis architecture. Both SpaceX and Blue Origin concurred with the new approach. Congressional stakeholders were briefed and expressed support.

events.title: Isaacman Outlines Post-Overhaul Architecture Details
events.detail: In a one-on-one interview, NASA Administrator Isaacman discussed Artemis II launch progress, NASA OIG readiness report responses, workforce planning, and upcoming milestones. He reiterated his target of two lunar landings in 2028, confirmed continued development of both SpaceX Starship HLS and Blue Origin Blue Moon Mk.2 landers, and indicated further details on the revised post-Artemis III architecture would be forthcoming in coming weeks.
